data_in.description: Tagged tabular file (CSV or TSV format) with export tags in column headers

data_out.description: Nested JSON object with table-record-field hierarchy matching tagged column structure

domain_knowledge: Export tag syntax uses # prefix for table identifiers and #. prefix for field names, with the format #<table_name>.id and #.<field_name> for denoting nested record structure.
Tagged columns must be parsed in order to establish hierarchical relationships between tables, records, and fields in the output JSON.
The parser acts as an intermediary between raw tagged tabular data and the Conversion Directives Engine, which expects a specific nested JSON structure.
Record identifiers within a table must be unique to avoid collisions when constructing the JSON hierarchy.

direct_checks: verify file exists: parser implementation in messes repository (github:MoseleyBioinformaticsLab/messes)
verify file_format_is: parser accepts tabular input with export tags matching pattern #<table_name>.id and #.<field_name>
verify script_runs: parser execution on tagged tabular test file without errors
verify output_matches_reference: generated JSON structure conforms to nested schema consumed by directive resolvers (byte-for-byte matching against reference conversion or schema validation)
verify field_present: output JSON contains expected nested fields corresponding to input table and field tags
verify format_is: output is valid JSON (parseable by standard JSON parser)
expert_review: assess correctness of tag interpretation logic: does parser correctly map export tags (#<table_name>.id, #.<field_name>) to nested JSON hierarchy
assess completeness of conversion: does generated JSON structure preserve all information from tagged input and match directives engine consumption expectations
assess handling of edge cases: nested tables, missing tags, malformed tags, duplicate field names

+--------+---------------------+-------------------+-------------------+
| #tags  | #<table_name_1>.id  | #.<field_name_1>  |'

cmd: import csv, json, sys; data = {}; f = open('{inputs.tagged_tabular_file}'); dialect = csv.Sniffer().sniff(f.read(1024)); f.seek(0); reader = csv.DictReader(f, dialect=dialect); headers = reader.fieldnames; [data.setdefault(h.split('.')[0] if '.' in h else h.split('#')[1].split('.')[0], {}).setdefault(h.split('#')[1].split('.')[0] if '#' in h and '.' in h.split('#')[1] else 'default', []).append({h.split('#')[1].split('.')[1]: row[h] for h in headers if h}) for row in reader]; print(json.dumps(data, indent=2))

task_description: Implement a parser that accepts tagged tabular input (using export tags like #<table_name>.id and #.<field_name>) and converts it to nested JSON structures compatible with the Conversion Directives Engine resolver.

task_inputs.label: Tagged tabular file with export tags in column headers

task_objective: Parse tagged tabular input using #<table_name>.id and #.<field_name> export tag columns and serialize to equivalent nested JSON structure consumable by conversion directive resolvers.

task_outputs.label: Nested JSON object ready for Conversion Directives Engine

title: Implement tagged-tabular-to-JSON conversion for Conversion Directives input parsing
uncertainty_notes: This card was generated by the LLM-assisted pipeline and needs scientific expert review.
Each TracedClaim's evidence_span has been substring-checked against its source section; see logs/llm_calls.jsonl and capsules/<task_id>/quality_report.json for groundedness results.
workflow_description: 1. Read tagged tabular file and parse row-by-row, identifying export tags (prefixed with # or #.) in column headers. 2. Scan the tag syntax to extract table name, field name, and record identifier from each tagged column. 3. Build a hierarchical JSON object structure by nesting field values under their corresponding table and record identifiers. 4. Validate that all tagged columns conform to the expected tag format and that no duplicate record identifiers exist within a table. 5. Output the nested JSON structure as a dictionary-like object ready for consumption by downstream directive resolvers.
